does: This paper shows that standard reinforcement learning post-training of language models already contains implicit step-level reward signals without requiring explicit reward model training. The authors derive a theoretical result proving that the log-probability ratio between an RL-trained policy and its reference policy—termed progress advantage—exactly recovers the optimal advantage function under general stochastic Markov decision processes. They demonstrate this signal is annotation-free and domain-agnostic, extractable as a byproduct of existing RL pipelines. The work validates progress advantage across three applications: test-time scaling for improved performance, uncertainty quantification for identifying model confidence, and failure attribution for understanding error sources on multiple benchmarks.
connects: This work extends recent advances in process reward models and step-level evaluation of language model reasoning, building on prior efforts to move beyond outcome-only supervision. It connects to the broader literature on advantage estimation in reinforcement learning and policy evaluation, adapting classical RL theory to the LLM domain. The paper responds to acknowledged challenges in agentic settings—long-horizon interactions, irreversible actions, and stochastic environment feedback—that have made traditional reward model training difficult. It builds on the understanding that RL post-training fundamentally changes model behavior and internal representations, enabling new forms of signal extraction without additional human annotation overhead.
enables: This work opens directions for more efficient post-training of agentic systems by eliminating the need for separate reward model construction. It enables better runtime capabilities for LLM agents through test-time scaling strategies grounded in theoretically justified advantage signals. The annotation-free nature of progress advantage could accelerate development of safer, more interpretable AI systems by providing built-in uncertainty and failure analysis without additional labeling costs. The framework suggests future work on using these implicit signals for online adaptation, multi-agent settings, and more sophisticated test-time computation allocation based on estimated progress. It also points toward richer understanding of what RL post-training learns and how to extract interpretable signals from trained policies.
